subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may calculate total numbers (1000) minus all-distinct digits (9×9×8×7 = 4536 is wrong approach for 4-digit numbers starting with 6). Confusing complementary counting steps leads to wrong answer.
elimination_hint: Use complementary counting: total 4-digit numbers from 6000–6999 = 1000. Subtract those with ALL digits distinct: 6___ where remaining 3 digits chosen from 0–9 excluding 6 = 9×8×7 = 504. So repeated = 1000–504 = 496.
factual_hooks: Total numbers 6000–6999 = 1000
All-distinct count = 9×8×7 = 504
contrast_with: Combination problems — order does not matter there

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement II sounds plausible since Lok Sabha is the more powerful house; students forget that Emergency proclamations require approval of both Houses, not just Lok Sabha.
elimination_hint: Rajya Sabha's special power under Article 249 (national interest legislation on State List) is well-established — Statement I is correct. Statement II is false because both Houses must approve Emergency proclamations.
factual_hooks: Article 249: Rajya Sabha can pass resolution for Parliament to legislate on State List
Emergency proclamation must be approved by both Houses (Article 352)
contrast_with: Rajya Sabha special powers vs ordinary legislation — distinction important
contemporary_relevance: Relevant when discussing federal balance and role of Rajya Sabha.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Diamantina sounds like it could be in the Atlantic (Brazilian origin) causing confusion; Pacific trenches (Mariana) are more famous, drawing answers toward Pacific.
elimination_hint: Diamantina Trench is located in the southeastern Indian Ocean near Australia; remember by associating Diamantina with the Diamantina River in Australia.
factual_hooks: Diamantina Trench is in the Indian Ocean
Located south of Australia
One of the deepest points in the Indian Ocean
contrast_with: Java/Sunda Trench — deepest point of Indian Ocean vs Diamantina

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may think FRBMA addresses only fiscal deficit (the more publicized target) and overlook its explicit revenue deficit targets.
elimination_hint: FRBMA 2003 mandates elimination of revenue deficit and reduction of fiscal deficit to 3% of GDP — both are explicitly covered, making option C correct.
factual_hooks: FRBMA 2003 covers both fiscal deficit and revenue deficit
Fiscal deficit target: 3% of GDP
Revenue deficit was to be eliminated under FRBMA
contrast_with: Fiscal deficit vs Revenue deficit — revenue deficit excludes capital items
contemporary_relevance: FRBMA remains relevant amid fiscal stimulus debates post-COVID.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement II about NREGA wage provision sounds correct intuitively, but the Act actually provides that wages paid shall NOT be less than minimum wage — the provision refers to agricultural minimum wage as the floor, not a mandate to pay exactly that rate.
elimination_hint: Bharat Nirman did include drinking water (Rajiv Gandhi National Drinking Water Mission component). For Statement II, NREGA wage = minimum wage for agricultural labourers in the State — this is actually correct per the Act, so revisit: Answer A (only I) implies II is wrong — check that NREGA set its own schedule of rates, not necessarily equal to agricultural minimum wage in all states.
factual_hooks: Bharat Nirman included rural drinking water as one of six components
NREGA wages were linked to minimum agricultural wages under the Act
NREGA 2005 guaranteed 100 days employment
contrast_with: Bharat Nirman vs MGNREGA — infrastructure vs employment guarantee
contemporary_relevance: MGNREGA remains key rural safety net; wage debates ongoing.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Assertion about Andhra Pradesh having less net sown area than West Bengal is actually false — Andhra Pradesh has a higher % net sown area, making A false. The reason about laterite soil is also an oversimplification.
elimination_hint: Andhra Pradesh actually has a higher proportion of net sown area than West Bengal; hence Assertion A is false. Even if R were true about laterite soil, A being false makes the answer D (A false, R true) — but laterite is not the dominant soil of AP either, so D is the answer.
factual_hooks: Andhra Pradesh has black (regur) and red soils, not predominantly laterite
West Bengal has alluvial and red soils with high cultivation intensity
Net sown area % varies by state geography
contrast_with: Laterite soil (Meghalaya, parts of AP hills) vs Black soil (Deccan plateau AP)

subject: History
trap_factor: The Assertion about Muhammad bin Tughlaq issuing a gold coin called Dinar is true. But the Reason is wrong — he issued token currency in COPPER/BRASS, not gold, to substitute silver tankas (not to promote West Asian trade).
elimination_hint: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's famous token currency experiment used bronze/copper coins, not gold. The Dinar was indeed a gold coin he issued but for different reasons. A is true, R is false — answer is C.
factual_hooks: Muhammad bin Tughlaq issued token bronze currency to replace silver tanka
Ibn Battutah called his gold coin 'Dinar'
Token currency failed due to counterfeiting
prerequisite_concepts: Token currency experiment of Muhammad bin Tughlaq
contrast_with: Token currency experiment vs gold Dinar — two different monetary measures

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Cellulose (as cellulose acetate) IS used in safety/shatter-resistant glass as an interlayer — so A is true. However, the Reason that polysaccharides are insoluble in water is false (many dissolve partially). Answer is D because A is false — cellulose acetate is used in photography film, not shatter-proof glass; PVB (polyvinyl butyral) is used.
elimination_hint: Shatter-proof (laminated) glass uses PVB (polyvinyl butyral) as interlayer, not cellulose. Assertion A is false. Reason R about polysaccharides insolubility is partially false (starch dissolves). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Shatter-proof glass uses PVB (polyvinyl butyral) interlayer
Cellulose is a polysaccharide but NOT used in shatter-proof glass
Most polysaccharides have limited water solubility
contrast_with: Cellulose acetate (film) vs PVB (safety glass)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Assertion (Mars takes less time to orbit than Earth) is FALSE — Mars is farther from the Sun and takes ~687 Earth days. The Reason about Mars's smaller diameter is TRUE but irrelevant to orbital period (which depends on orbital radius).
elimination_hint: Orbital period depends on distance from Sun (Kepler's third law), not diameter. Mars is farther from Sun → longer orbital period. A is false, R is true → Answer D.
factual_hooks: Mars orbital period = 687 Earth days (longer than Earth's 365 days)
Kepler's Third Law: orbital period increases with distance from Sun
Mars diameter ≈ 53% of Earth's diameter (R is true)
contrast_with: Orbital period (distance-dependent) vs rotation period (planet-size-related)
contemporary_relevance: Mars missions make Solar System knowledge highly relevant.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Assertion A states every State has a High Court in its territory — this is FALSE as some states share High Courts (e.g., Punjab & Haryana share Chandigarh HC). Reason R states Constitution provides HC in each State — also misleading.
elimination_hint: Not every state has its own High Court — some share (Punjab-Haryana, Maharashtra-Goa). Assertion A is false. The Constitution allows for common HCs for two or more states. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Article 214: Each State shall have a High Court — but Parliament can establish common HC (Article 231)
Punjab and Haryana share a common High Court at Chandigarh
Some UTs also fall under jurisdiction of nearby State HCs
contrast_with: Article 214 (state HC) vs Article 231 (common HC for two+ states)
contemporary_relevance: Debates around judicial vacancies and HC jurisdictions remain relevant.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Both statements appear true and the reason seems to explain the assertion well. Ensure you verify that BoP truly includes invisible items (services, remittances) while BoT does not — this is the exact distinction.
elimination_hint: BoP = BoT (visible) + invisible items (services, remittances, capital flows). This directly explains why BoP gives a fuller picture. Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A → Answer A.
factual_hooks: Balance of Payments includes both current and capital account
Balance of Trade covers only visible (merchandise) exports and imports
Invisible items include services, remittances, financial transfers
contrast_with: Balance of Trade (narrow) vs Balance of Payments (comprehensive)
contemporary_relevance: India's BoP management and current account deficit are perennial UPSC topics.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Bangladesh is geographically close to Southeast Asia and shares borders with Myanmar (an ASEAN member), making it a plausible distractor. Students often confuse SAARC membership with ASEAN.
elimination_hint: ASEAN has 10 members: Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam. Bangladesh is a SAARC member, not ASEAN.
factual_hooks: ASEAN has 10 member states
Bangladesh is in SAARC, not ASEAN
Myanmar joined ASEAN in 1997
contrast_with: SAARC (South Asia) vs ASEAN (Southeast Asia)
contemporary_relevance: India's Act East Policy makes ASEAN membership knowledge essential.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement I wrongly says INSAT-4A was launched from New Mexico (it was launched from Kourou, French Guiana by Arianespace). Students may not know the launch site precisely.
elimination_hint: INSAT-4A was launched from Kourou (French Guiana), not New Mexico. Arianespace (European provider) did launch it. Tata Sky did use INSAT-4A for DTH. So II and III are correct → Answer C.
factual_hooks: INSAT-4A launched by Arianespace from Kourou, French Guiana (Dec 2005)
Tata Sky used INSAT-4A for DTH services
Arianespace is a European commercial launch provider
contrast_with: ISRO-launched satellites vs commercially-launched Indian satellites

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse cornea with lens or think the 'whole eye' is donated. The lens has its own blood supply issues and retina cannot be transplanted currently.
elimination_hint: Only the cornea is transplanted in eye donation — it is avascular (no blood supply) making rejection less likely. Lens, retina and whole eye are not transplantable.
factual_hooks: Cornea is the only part transplanted in eye donation
Cornea is avascular — low rejection risk
Retina and lens cannot be transplanted
contrast_with: Cornea transplant (feasible) vs retinal transplant (not currently standard)
contemporary_relevance: Organ donation awareness remains a public health priority.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: CMYK sounds like a technical code that could relate to electronics or navigation; students unfamiliar with printing technology may guess randomly.
elimination_hint: CMYK stands for Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (Black) — the four ink colors used in color offset printing. This is a standard printing industry term.
factual_hooks: CMYK = Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key Black — used in offset/color printing
Contrast with RGB used in digital screens
CMYK is subtractive color model
contrast_with: CMYK (print/subtractive) vs RGB (digital/additive)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Kofi Annan was from Ghana, not Nigeria — these West African countries are easily confused. Statement I is therefore wrong about him being from Nigeria.
elimination_hint: Kofi Annan was from Ghana (not Nigeria). Statement I is false. Statements II (first from UN staff ranks) and III (served two terms) are both correct → Answer C.
factual_hooks: Kofi Annan was from Ghana, 7th UN Secretary-General
First UN Secretary-General appointed from within UN staff
Served two terms: 1997–2006
contrast_with: Ghana vs Nigeria — both West African but distinct nations

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: This is a current-affairs question from 2006 about FIFA World Cup — dated and unlikely to recur. Russia did not qualify for the 2006 World Cup, making it the correct answer.
elimination_hint: Russia failed to qualify for the 2006 FIFA World Cup in Germany. Togo, Ivory Coast, and Switzerland all participated. Answer: A (Russia).
factual_hooks: 2006 FIFA World Cup held in Germany
Russia did not qualify for 2006 World Cup
Togo and Ivory Coast made their notable World Cup debuts in 2006

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: The French name 'Medecins Sans Frontieres' (Doctors Without Borders) may mislead students toward IOC or EU connections due to its European origin.
elimination_hint: MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières / Doctors Without Borders) is an international humanitarian NGO providing emergency medical care in conflict zones and disasters. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: MSF = Médecins Sans Frontières = Doctors Without Borders
International humanitarian NGO, Nobel Peace Prize 1999
Operates in conflict zones and disaster areas
contrast_with: MSF (NGO, medical humanitarian) vs WHO (IGO, health policy)
contemporary_relevance: MSF's role in humanitarian crises (Syria, Ukraine, Gaza) remains prominent.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: ELISA is a well-known test; students may assume it's used for cancer (it is used for HIV/AIDS detection). About 85% of humans are Rh+ (not 50%), making Statement II clearly wrong.
elimination_hint: ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay) is used primarily for detecting HIV, not cancer (cancer uses biopsy, tumor markers etc.). ~85% are Rh+, not 50%. Both statements are false → Answer D.
factual_hooks: ELISA is primarily used to detect HIV/AIDS, not cancer
~85% of humans are Rh positive, ~15% Rh negative
Cancer detection uses biopsy, CT scans, tumor markers
contrast_with: ELISA (immunological detection) vs biopsy (tissue-based cancer detection)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: The Nordic Council is associated with Scandinavian/Nordic nations. UK is a European power but not Scandinavian; however, students might think it participates due to proximity.
elimination_hint: Nordic Council members: Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden (and autonomous territories like Greenland, Faroe Islands, Åland). UK is not a member.
factual_hooks: Nordic Council founded 1952, 5 full member states
UK is not part of Nordic/Scandinavian group
Nordic Council focuses on cooperation among Nordic nations
contrast_with: Nordic Council (Nordic nations only) vs Council of Europe (broader European membership)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Without the actual network diagram this question cannot be fully solved; students must systematically enumerate paths. Guessing between 28–35 is common without systematic approach.
elimination_hint: For path-counting in networks, use systematic enumeration from node A to B counting all possible non-repeating routes through intermediate stations. The answer is 31.
factual_hooks: Path counting requires systematic enumeration
Each unique route between A and B counts separately
Answer = 31 paths
contrast_with: Path counting (all routes) vs shortest path (optimization)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Macedonia is in the Balkans; students may confuse the regional dispute over Macedonian territory with Balkan wars involving Romania or Portugal.
elimination_hint: The historical Macedonia region spans parts of modern Greece (Greek Macedonia) and North Macedonia; Bulgaria also claims historical Macedonian heritage. The key dispute is between Bulgaria and Greece.
factual_hooks: Historical Macedonia territory contested between Greece and Bulgaria (and North Macedonia)
Greece objects to use of 'Macedonia' name — resolved via Prespa Agreement 2018
Macedonian Question has been a Balkan flashpoint for over a century
contrast_with: Modern North Macedonia (state) vs historical Macedonian territory (larger region)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Setting up algebraic variables for 'only tea', 'only coffee', 'both' is tricky; students may confuse 'takes tea' (includes both) with 'takes only tea'.
elimination_hint: Let only-tea = t, only-coffee = c, both = b. Tea drinkers = t+b = 2c; Coffee drinkers = c+b = 2t. Solving: Statement I (sum = 4×both) needs checking; Statement II (only-coffee + only-tea = 2×both) check algebraically. Only Statement II is correct.
factual_hooks: Use variables: only-tea=t, only-coffee=c, both=b
Given: t+b=2c and c+b=2t, solve for ratios
Statement II: t+c=2b is verifiable from equations
contrast_with: 'Takes tea' (inclusive of both) vs 'takes only tea' (exclusive)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Kye Monastery is often associated with Spiti Valley (Himachal Pradesh), not Arunachal Pradesh. Students may confuse Buddhist monasteries in North-East India with Himalayan monasteries.
elimination_hint: Kye (Ki) Monastery is in Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh — NOT Arunachal Pradesh. Hence option D (Kye Monastery: Arunachal Pradesh) is the incorrectly matched pair.
factual_hooks: Kye Monastery is in Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh
Rumtek Monastery is in Sikkim (correct)
Tabo Monastery is in Himachal Pradesh (correct)
Dhankar Monastery is in Himachal Pradesh (correct)
contrast_with: Himachal Pradesh monasteries (Kye, Tabo, Dhankar) vs Arunachal Pradesh monasteries (Tawang)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Confusing Dzukou Valley (Nagaland-Manipur border) with Sikkim, or Sangla Valley with Jammu & Kashmir vs Himachal Pradesh creates multiple wrong combinations.
elimination_hint: Markha Valley → J&K (Ladakh); Dzukou Valley → Nagaland; Sangla Valley → Himachal Pradesh; Yumthang Valley → Sikkim. Match: I-C, II-D, III-B, IV-A → Answer D.
factual_hooks: Markha Valley: Ladakh (J&K)
Dzukou Valley: Nagaland
Sangla Valley: Himachal Pradesh (Kinnaur)
Yumthang Valley: Sikkim
contrast_with: Kullu Valley (HP) vs Sangla Valley (HP) — both in Himachal but different districts

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Cauvery River rising in Andhra Pradesh sounds plausible due to its flow through the state, but it actually originates in Karnataka (Coorg/Talakaveri).
elimination_hint: Cauvery rises at Talakaveri in Kodagu (Coorg) district of Karnataka, NOT Andhra Pradesh. Other options are correct: Mahanadi (Chhattisgarh), Godavari (Maharashtra), Tapti (MP).
factual_hooks: Cauvery rises at Talakaveri, Kodagu, Karnataka
Mahanadi originates in Chhattisgarh (Amarkantak area)
Godavari rises in Nashik, Maharashtra
Tapti rises in Madhya Pradesh
contrast_with: Cauvery (Karnataka origin) vs Krishna (Maharashtra origin, Mahabaleshwar)
contemporary_relevance: Cauvery water dispute between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu remains active.

subject: History
trap_factor: Confusing Sikandar Shah's position in the dynasty — whether he came before or after Bahlol Lodi — is the main trap. Ibrahim Lodi is well-known as the last Lodi ruler defeated at Panipat.
elimination_hint: Lodi dynasty order: Bahlol Khan Lodi (1451–89) → Sikandar Shah (1489–1517) → Ibrahim Lodi (1517–26). Correct chronological order → Answer C.
factual_hooks: Bahlol Khan Lodi founded Lodi dynasty (1451)
Sikandar Lodi built Agra as new capital
Ibrahim Lodi defeated at First Battle of Panipat (1526) by Babur
contrast_with: Sayyid dynasty (preceded Lodis) vs Lodi dynasty

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Without the actual cube diagrams this is hard to verify — students may not systematically track which faces are visible in each position. All three statements being correct (D) might seem too easy.
elimination_hint: For cube face-opposite problems, use position data from multiple views to deduce opposite faces. With sufficient views (Figures I–IV), all three statements about opposite faces can be verified → Answer D.
factual_hooks: Cube: 6 faces, 3 pairs of opposite faces
Two views are typically sufficient to determine one pair of opposite faces
Systematically eliminate adjacent faces to find opposites
contrast_with: Adjacent faces vs opposite faces in a cube

subject: History
trap_factor: Lord Dalhousie is associated with the period just before 1857 (Doctrine of Lapse, annexations) leading to the revolt, making him a plausible wrong answer.
elimination_hint: Lord Canning was Governor-General during the 1857 Sepoy Mutiny and became the first Viceroy of India in 1858. Dalhousie's provocative policies preceded it but he left India before 1857.
factual_hooks: Lord Canning was Governor-General during 1857 Revolt
Canning became first Viceroy of India (1858)
Lord Dalhousie's Doctrine of Lapse preceded 1857
related_concepts: Transfer of power from Company to Crown
contrast_with: Lord Dalhousie (before 1857, caused grievances) vs Lord Canning (during 1857)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement I says Cama unfurled the flag at Paris — actually it was Stuttgart (International Socialist Congress, 1907), not Paris. This location error is the key trap.
elimination_hint: Cama unfurled the flag at Stuttgart (Germany) in 1907, not Paris. Statement I is false. She was private secretary to Dadabhai Naoroji (II true) and born to Parsi parents (III true). Answer: B (II and III only).
factual_hooks: Bhikaji Cama unfurled Indian flag at Stuttgart, Germany (1907)
She was private secretary to Dadabhai Naoroji
Born into a Parsi family in Bombay
contrast_with: Stuttgart (1907 flag) vs Paris (Cama's base of operations) — different events

subject: History
trap_factor: Ramakrishna Paramhansa was Vivekananda's guru — students may attribute Vivekananda's travelogue to him. Annie Besant is associated with Theosophical Society travels.
elimination_hint: 'Lectures from Colombo to Almora' documents Swami Vivekananda's triumphant return to India after the Chicago Parliament of Religions (1893). It covers his lectures from 1897.
factual_hooks: 'Lectures from Colombo to Almora' by Swami Vivekananda (1897)
Records his return journey to India after Chicago Parliament of Religions
Published as part of The Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda
contrast_with: Vivekananda (Lectures from Colombo to Almora) vs his Chicago speech (Parliament of Religions)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may confuse circular motion speeds and not correctly calculate relative velocities on the circle. The equidistant points setup requires careful arc-length speed calculation.
elimination_hint: Person 1: covers 2 segments in time t (A to C). Person 2: covers 3 segments in time t (B to E). Speeds are in ratio 2:3. Find time to meet using relative speed = 1 segment/t. They meet after 7t. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: 8 equidistant points on circle = 8 equal segments
Speed ratio = 2:3 (person 1 covers 2 units, person 2 covers 3 units per t)
Relative speed = 1 unit per t; gap = 7 units → meet at 7t
contrast_with: Linear chase problems vs circular chase problems

subject: Economy
trap_factor: SBI is India's largest bank and might seem a logical parent for NHB. ICICI and LIC are also large financial institutions, creating confusion.
elimination_hint: National Housing Bank was established in 1988 as a wholly-owned subsidiary of RBI under the National Housing Bank Act. It was later de-linked from RBI in 2019.
factual_hooks: NHB established 1988 as RBI subsidiary
NHB is apex institution for housing finance in India
NHB was delinked from RBI in 2019 and placed under Government
contrast_with: NHB (housing finance) vs NABARD (agricultural finance) — both DFIs under RBI heritage
contemporary_relevance: Housing finance regulation and NHB's role in affordable housing remain relevant.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement I wrongly says 75th Amendment made education a Fundamental Right — it was the 86th Amendment (2002). Students mixing amendment numbers is the primary trap.
elimination_hint: 86th Amendment (2002) inserted Article 21A (Right to Education for 6-14 years). 42nd Amendment (1976) added Education to Concurrent List — Statement III is correct. Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan focus on computer education (II) is debatable. Answer C (II and III only) means I is wrong.
factual_hooks: 86th Amendment 2002 added Article 21A (RTE)
42nd Amendment 1976 moved Education to Concurrent List
Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan launched 2001 for universal elementary education
contrast_with: 86th Amendment (RTE Fundamental Right) vs 42nd Amendment (Education to Concurrent List)
contemporary_relevance: RTE implementation and NEP 2020 make education policy highly relevant.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may compute 5^4 = 625 total ways and subtract 1 (all correct) = 624. Some may incorrectly compute 4^4 or make off-by-one errors.
elimination_hint: Total ways to answer 4 questions with 5 choices each = 5^4 = 625. Ways to get ALL correct = 1. Ways NOT to get all correct = 625 – 1 = 624. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Total combinations = 5^4 = 625
Subtract 1 (all-correct scenario)
Answer = 624
contrast_with: At least one wrong (complementary) vs exactly one wrong (direct)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Ela Bhatt's work is in the women's labour sector (SEWA) — this must be matched correctly. Confusion between Mahasweta Devi (literature/social work) and Ebrahim Alkazi (theatre) is common.
elimination_hint: Ela Bhatt → SEWA (women's labour). Mahasweta Devi → literature + tribal social work. Ebrahim Alkazi → theatre training (NSD director). Vijay Govindarajan → management teaching. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Ela Bhatt founded SEWA (Self Employed Women's Association)
Mahasweta Devi: Bengali author and activist for tribals
Ebrahim Alkazi: Director, National School of Drama
Vijay Govindarajan: management professor, Dartmouth
contrast_with: Ela Bhatt (labour activism) vs Mahasweta Devi (literary activism)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Common bile duct emptying into the stomach sounds plausible — bile is associated with digestion which starts in stomach. But bile is needed to emulsify fats in the small intestine (duodenum).
elimination_hint: Common bile duct opens into the duodenum (part of small intestine) at the hepatopancreatic ampulla, NOT the stomach. Pancreatic duct also opens into the duodenum. Statement I is false, II is true. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Common bile duct opens into duodenum at hepatopancreatic ampulla (Ampulla of Vater)
Pancreatic duct also opens into duodenum
Bile emulsifies fats in small intestine, not stomach
contrast_with: Stomach (acid digestion) vs Duodenum (bile + enzyme digestion)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Participatory Notes involve foreign investors; students may associate them with IMF/World Bank or domestic institutions like Consolidated Fund.
elimination_hint: Participatory Notes (P-Notes) are derivative instruments issued by registered Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs) to overseas investors not registered with SEBI. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: P-Notes are issued by FIIs to foreign investors not SEBI-registered
P-Notes were subject to regulatory scrutiny due to anonymity concerns
SEBI tightened P-Note regulations multiple times
contrast_with: FDI (direct ownership) vs FII/P-Notes (portfolio/derivative)
contemporary_relevance: P-Note regulation remains relevant to capital market oversight debates.

subject: History
trap_factor: Rajendra Prasad is associated with the Congress and became President of India; students may associate him with Defence or External Affairs rather than the less glamorous Food and Agriculture portfolio.
elimination_hint: In the Interim Government (1946), Rajendra Prasad held the Food and Agriculture portfolio. Jawaharlal Nehru held External Affairs; Sardar Patel held Home; Baldev Singh held Defence.
factual_hooks: Rajendra Prasad: Food and Agriculture in Interim Government 1946
Jawaharlal Nehru: External Affairs and Commonwealth Relations
Interim Government formed September 1946 under British paramountcy
contrast_with: Constituent Assembly role vs Interim Government executive role of leaders

subject: Governance
trap_factor: All four options are renowned Indian classical dancers, making this highly specific current-affairs trivia from 2005. Any could plausibly have won such an award.
elimination_hint: Yamini Krishnamurthy, the renowned Bharatanatyam and Kuchipudi dancer, received the Sangeet Natak Akademi Ratna Award (Sangeet Natak Akademi Fellowship) in 2005 for lifetime achievement.
factual_hooks: Yamini Krishnamurthy: Bharatanatyam/Kuchipudi maestro
Sangeet Natak Akademi Fellowship = highest honour by the academy
Chandralekha and Balasaraswati also legendary dancers
contrast_with: Sangeet Natak Akademi Fellowship (lifetime) vs Sangeet Natak Akademi Award (annual)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement I about permanent HC judges not pleading before any court except SC sounds correct — but they can practice before other High Courts (not the one from which they retired). Statement II about 5-year judicial office qualification is one of two qualifications (the other is 10-year HC advocate).
elimination_hint: Article 220: A retired permanent HC judge cannot plead in the HC they served or courts subordinate to it, but CAN plead in other HCs and SC. Statement I overstates the restriction. Article 217 requires 10-year advocate experience in HC or 10-year judicial office. Statement II says 'five years' which is wrong. Both false → D.
factual_hooks: Article 220: Retired HC judges cannot practice in their own HC or subordinate courts
Article 217: HC judge must have 10 years of HC advocacy or held judicial office
Retired HC judges CAN practice in Supreme Court
contrast_with: SC judge retirement restrictions (Article 124) vs HC judge restrictions (Article 220)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Without the figure showing arrangement squares, full solving is not possible. Note idx 41 has correct answer A (38) while idx 46 has the same question with answer B (28) — a duplicate with different claimed answers.
elimination_hint: For placing 6 balls in one row and 2 in another out of available squares: systematically count combinations of which squares are filled. The answer here is given as 38.
factual_hooks: Combinatorial arrangement problems require visualization of available squares
Count valid configurations for 6+2 ball placement
Answer given = 38 for this variant
contrast_with: Permutation (ordered) vs Combination (unordered) arrangements

subject: History
trap_factor: Greeks and Romans had contact with India; Chinese travelers like Xuanzang visited India; all seem plausible. The term 'Hindu' derived from the Persian/Arabic pronunciation of 'Sindhu' river.
elimination_hint: The word 'Hindu' was first used by Arabs (and Persians) referring to people east of the Indus (Sindhu) river. Arab geographers used 'Al-Hind' for India. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: 'Hindu' derives from Persian/Arabic 'Sindhu' → 'Hindu' (people of the Indus region)
Arabs first used 'Hindu' as a geographic-ethnic descriptor
Greeks called the region 'Indos/India'
prerequisite_concepts: Ancient contacts between India and outside world
contrast_with: 'Hindu' (Arab/Persian origin) vs 'India/Indos' (Greek origin)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Slovenia's capital is Ljubljana, not Bratislava. Bratislava is the capital of Slovakia. This Slovenia-Slovakia confusion is a very common error.
elimination_hint: Bratislava is the capital of Slovakia, not Slovenia. Slovenia's capital is Ljubljana. Therefore the pair Slovenia: Bratislava is incorrectly matched. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Slovenia capital = Ljubljana
Slovakia capital = Bratislava
Slovenia and Slovakia are frequently confused
contrast_with: Slovenia (former Yugoslavia, Adriatic) vs Slovakia (former Czechoslovakia, Central Europe)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Wole Soyinka is a Nigerian name; students may confuse him with an African politician or economist rather than a literary figure.
elimination_hint: Wole Soyinka is a Nigerian playwright, poet and novelist who won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1986 — the first African to win this prize.
factual_hooks: Wole Soyinka: Nigerian author, Nobel Prize in Literature 1986
First African to win Nobel Prize in Literature
Known for works like 'Death and the King's Horseman'
contrast_with: Wole Soyinka (literature) vs Kofi Annan (UN/diplomacy) — both prominent Africans

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement II about Harmattan being an East African coast wind is wrong — it's a West African dry wind. Statement III about Greece and Albania being part of the Iberian Peninsula is wrong — Iberian Peninsula = Spain and Portugal.
elimination_hint: Statement I is true (nautical mile > terrestrial/land mile: 1 nautical mile = 1.852 km, 1 land mile = 1.609 km). Harmattan blows in West Africa (not East). Greece/Albania are NOT on Iberian Peninsula. Only I is correct → Answer D.
factual_hooks: 1 nautical mile = 1.852 km; 1 statute (land) mile = 1.609 km
Harmattan: hot, dry, dusty wind blowing from Sahara across West Africa
Iberian Peninsula = Spain and Portugal only
contrast_with: Harmattan (West Africa) vs Khamsin/Simoom (East Africa/Middle East) — different regional dry winds

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Without the figure, exact count is difficult. Note this is a near-duplicate of idx 41 with a different correct answer (28 vs 38), suggesting different grid configurations.
elimination_hint: Systematically count configurations for placing 6 balls in one horizontal row and 2 in another from available squares. For this grid configuration, answer = 28.
factual_hooks: Count valid row combinations from available grid squares
6+2 = 8 balls total in 2 rows
Answer = 28 for this figure
contrast_with: Different grid sizes give different answer counts

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Confusing Amartya Sen's book — 'The Argumentative Indian' vs 'Development as Freedom'; Bimal Jalan wrote 'The Future of India'; Arundhati Roy is known for fiction but also political essays.
elimination_hint: Amartya Sen → 'The Argumentative Indian' (B); Bimal Jalan → 'The Future of India' (C); Arundhati Roy → 'An Ordinary Person's Guide to Empire' (A); Mani Shankar Aiyar → 'Confessions of a Secular Fundamentalist' (D). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: 'The Argumentative Indian' by Amartya Sen (2005)
'The Future of India' by Bimal Jalan, former RBI Governor
Arundhati Roy known for 'God of Small Things' and political essays
prerequisite_concepts: Prominent Indian authors and their works
contrast_with: Amartya Sen (academic economist-writer) vs Bimal Jalan (central banker-writer)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Both statements are specific data-based claims from 2004-05 that require precise knowledge. India's export share crossing 2% of world merchandise exports in 2005 was not yet achieved (India's share was ~0.8–1% then).
elimination_hint: India's exports in 2004-05 grew by over 20% (not below 10%), making Statement I false. India's merchandise export share was ~1% in 2005, not 2%. But answer is A (only I correct) suggesting I may actually be correct about the specific year's growth rate in rupee terms. This is a data-specific question — answer: A.
factual_hooks: India's merchandise export share in world trade was ~1% in mid-2000s
WTO data showed India not crossing 2% share in 2005
Export growth rates varied significantly year-on-year
contrast_with: Value of exports (absolute) vs share in world trade (relative)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Meningococcal Meningitis is transmitted person-to-person via respiratory droplets (not mosquito bites). Students may confuse it with malaria or dengue which are mosquito-borne.
elimination_hint: Meningococcal Meningitis spreads through respiratory droplets (coughing, sneezing), NOT mosquito bites — Statement I is false. Vomiting and neck stiffness (neck pain) ARE classic symptoms — Statement II is true. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Meningococcal Meningitis: caused by Neisseria meningitidis
Transmission: respiratory droplets, NOT mosquitoes
Classic triad: fever, severe headache, neck stiffness
contrast_with: Meningitis (airborne) vs malaria/dengue (mosquito-borne)
contemporary_relevance: Meningitis remains a significant public health concern in sub-Saharan Africa and globally.

subject: History
trap_factor: Rashbehari Bose is associated with the Indian National Army in Southeast Asia and is an easy confusion with Subhas Chandra Bose. Lala Hardayal formed Ghadar Party.
elimination_hint: Subhas Chandra Bose raised the 'Free India Legion' (Indische Legion) in Germany/Europe from Indian POWs. Rashbehari Bose organized INA in Southeast Asia (different from Free Indian Legion).
factual_hooks: Free India Legion (Indische Legion) raised by Netaji in Europe 1942
Distinct from Rashbehari Bose's INA in Southeast Asia
Free India Legion soldiers were Indian POWs captured in North Africa
prerequisite_concepts: Subhas Chandra Bose's activities in Europe
contrast_with: Free India Legion (Germany/Europe, Subhas Bose) vs INA/Azad Hind Fauj (Southeast Asia, Rashbehari then Subhas Bose)

subject: History
trap_factor: Raja Rao is a famous Indian English novelist (not Telugu) — students may assume he writes in his native regional language. His famous works like 'Kanthapura' are in English.
elimination_hint: Raja Rao was an Indian writer in English, not Telugu. The pair 'Raja Rao – Telugu' is incorrectly matched. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Raja Rao wrote in English (Kanthapura, The Serpent and the Rope)
Kannada is his mother tongue, but he wrote in English
Gobind Trimbuk Deshpande: Marathi (correct)
contrast_with: Raja Rao (English, Kannada origin) vs Shivaram Karanth (Kannada language)

subject: History
trap_factor: Aryabhata (Earth's orbit time) is often confused with digit zero invention; Brahmagupta formally defined zero arithmetically; Budhayana is credited with an early approximation of pi.
elimination_hint: Earth's orbit time → Aryabhata (A); Value of pi → Budhayana (C) — early approximation; Digit zero → Aryabhata is often cited but here matched to B (Bhaskaracharya) in the answer; Snakes & Ladders → Gyandev (D). Answer: C (I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-D).
factual_hooks: Aryabhata calculated Earth's rotation and orbital period
Budhayana gave early approximation of π
Digit zero credited to Aryabhata/Brahmagupta/Bhaskaracharya — contested
Snakes and Ladders originated in India, attributed to Gyandev
contrast_with: Aryabhata (astronomy + zero) vs Brahmagupta (formal zero rules) vs Bhaskaracharya (calculus precursor)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse Earth's orbital speed with rotational speed or escape velocity. The approximate 30 km/s figure is not intuitive.
elimination_hint: Earth's mean orbital velocity around the Sun is approximately 29.78 km/s ≈ 30 km/s. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Earth's orbital speed ≈ 30 km/s (29.78 km/s)
Earth completes one orbit in ~365.25 days
Orbital speed = 2π × 150 million km ÷ 365.25 days
contrast_with: Orbital speed (30 km/s) vs Earth's rotational speed at equator (~0.465 km/s)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Brazil is clearly on the Equator (true), but Zambia (option A) is NOT on the Equator — it's south of it. Sudan (option C) is not on the Equator. Venezuela (option D) is just north of the Equator.
elimination_hint: Equator passes through: Colombia, Kenya, and Indonesia (and also Brazil, Ecuador, Gabon, DRC, etc.). Option B (Colombia, Kenya, Indonesia) is correct.
factual_hooks: Equator countries (0°): Ecuador, Colombia, Brazil, Gabon, DRC, Kenya, Uganda, Indonesia
Kenya straddles Equator; Nairobi is near Equator
Colombia (South America), Kenya (Africa), Indonesia (Asia) all on Equator
prerequisite_concepts: Countries on Equator in each continent
contrast_with: Equatorial countries vs Tropical countries (within 23.5° of Equator)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: LIC being the 'oldest insurance company' is false — Oriental Insurance (1818) is among the oldest. National Insurance Company was nationalized in 1972. United India HQ at Chennai is correct.
elimination_hint: LIC (est. 1956) is not the oldest insurance company in India — Oriental Life Insurance (1818) predates it. Statements II and III are correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: LIC established 1956 — not the oldest insurance company
National Insurance Company nationalized 1972 as GIC subsidiary
United India Insurance HQ: Chennai (correct)
contrast_with: LIC (life insurance, 1956) vs Oriental Insurance (general, oldest) vs National Insurance (1906, nationalized 1972)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 (CO2 escaping to stratosphere) sounds scientific but CO2 doesn't selectively migrate to stratosphere. Statement 3 (polar ice trapping air) is about air bubbles in ice, not removing CO2 from atmosphere.
elimination_hint: Phytoplankton in oceans perform photosynthesis and absorb significant CO2 — Statement 2 is correct. CO2 doesn't escape to outer stratosphere (1 false), and polar ice caps don't remove CO2 (3 false). Answer: B (2 only).
factual_hooks: Phytoplankton account for ~50% of global photosynthesis
Oceans absorb ~30% of anthropogenic CO2 emissions
Polar ice caps trap ancient air bubbles (not a CO2 sink mechanism)
contrast_with: Ocean carbon sink (phytoplankton + dissolution) vs terrestrial carbon sink (forests)
contemporary_relevance: Critical topic for UNFCCC negotiations and climate action discussions.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: IMF is frequently confused as a World Bank constituent. The World Bank Group has 5 agencies; IMF is separate. Students often club IMF with World Bank as 'Bretton Woods institutions'.
elimination_hint: World Bank Group = IBRD + IDA + IFC + MIGA + ICSID. IMF is a separate Bretton Woods institution, NOT part of World Bank. IBRD, IFC, and IDA (I, II, III) constitute the World Bank. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: World Bank Group: IBRD, IDA, IFC, MIGA, ICSID
IMF is a separate institution from World Bank
Both IMF and World Bank created at Bretton Woods Conference (1944)
contrast_with: IMF (balance of payments support) vs World Bank (development financing)
contemporary_relevance: World Bank and IMF roles in developing country debt and development remain central.

subject: History
trap_factor: Qutb-ud-din Aibak's death via battle or political intrigue seems more fitting for a Sultanate ruler; death by horse fall while playing polo (Chaugan) is an unusual but historically documented cause.
elimination_hint: Qutb-ud-din Aibak died in 1210 after falling from his horse while playing Chaugan (polo) at Lahore. This is a well-documented historical fact. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Qutb-ud-din Aibak died playing Chaugan (polo) — fell from horse (1210)
Aibak founded Slave/Mamluk dynasty, Delhi Sultanate
He was not killed in battle — a rare peaceful(ish) death for a Sultan
contrast_with: Aibak (polo accident) vs Iltutmish (natural death) vs Balban (natural death)

subject: History
factual_hooks: Post-Aurangzeb Mughal decline led to fragmented polity by 1740s–1750s
Rise of regional powers: Marathas, Nawabs, Hyderabad, Mysore
European trading companies exploited political vacuum
contrast_with: Mughal centralized power (17th c.) vs fragmented regional polities (mid-18th c.)
mains_themes: Causes of British success in India
mains_intro: Mid-eighteenth century India witnessed a profound political crisis as the once-mighty Mughal Empire fragmented into competing regional power centres.
mains_body_points: Aurangzeb's prolonged Deccan campaigns depleted Mughal treasury and military strength, triggering rapid succession crises after 1707
Regional successor states emerged — Hyderabad, Awadh, Bengal — each functioning as de facto independent units with own armies and revenues
Maratha confederacy expanded aggressively but lacked unified command, creating instability rather than replacing Mughal order
Afghan invasions (Nadir Shah 1739, Ahmad Shah Abdali) further destroyed Mughal prestige and military capacity
European companies, especially EIC, exploited political vacuum by offering military alliances to regional powers, gaining territorial and commercial privileges
mains_conclusion: The fragmented polity of mid-18th century India created the structural conditions that enabled British colonial expansion through divide-and-rule strategies.
mains_interlinkages: Economy: Economic impact of British rule
contemporary_relevance: Debates on federalism, regional autonomy and national unity draw historical parallels.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Andaman & Nicobar Islands are geographically close to both the Madras and Calcutta jurisdictions. Students may assume Madras HC (Chennai) due to proximity to South India/Bay of Bengal.
elimination_hint: Andaman & Nicobar Islands fall under the territorial jurisdiction of the Calcutta High Court (historically administered from Bengal). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Andaman & Nicobar Islands: under Calcutta High Court jurisdiction
Historical administrative link to Bengal Presidency
Lakshadweep: under Kerala High Court; Puducherry: under Madras HC
contrast_with: Andaman & Nicobar (Calcutta HC) vs Lakshadweep (Kerala HC) vs Puducherry (Madras HC)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 sounds intuitive (both at 100°C) but steam has more heat than water at same temperature (latent heat). Statement 2 claims latent heats of fusion and vaporization are equal — they are NOT (vaporization >> fusion).
elimination_hint: Steam at 100°C has more heat than water at 100°C (latent heat of vaporization = 540 cal/g vs fusion = 80 cal/g). Statements 1 and 2 are false. Statement 3 (AC principle: evaporator absorbs heat, condenser releases heat) is correct. Answer: D (only 3).
factual_hooks: Latent heat of vaporization of water = 540 cal/g; fusion of ice = 80 cal/g
Steam at 100°C has MORE heat than boiling water at 100°C
Air conditioner: evaporator coils absorb heat; condenser coils reject heat
contrast_with: Latent heat of vaporization (high, 540 cal/g) vs latent heat of fusion (low, 80 cal/g)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 3 claims sugar can ONLY be made from sugarcane — this is clearly false as sugar beet is a major sugar source globally. Students may not think this through if focused on India.
elimination_hint: Molasses is a by-product of sugar production (I — true). Bagasse is used as boiler fuel in sugar mills (II — true). Sugar can also be made from sugar beet, not just sugarcane (III — false). Answer: A (I and II only).
factual_hooks: Molasses: dark viscous by-product, used in alcohol/ethanol production
Bagasse: fibrous residue after juice extraction, used as fuel
Sugar beet accounts for ~20% of global sugar production
contrast_with: Sugarcane-based sugar (tropical) vs Sugar beet-based sugar (temperate)
contemporary_relevance: Ethanol blending programme uses molasses — directly relevant to India's energy policy.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Precise longitude/latitude comparisons of Indian cities require map knowledge. Jabalpur (79.9°E) is between Indore (75.8°E) and Bhopal (77.4°E) — this is false (Jabalpur is east of both).
elimination_hint: Statement 1: Jabalpur (79.9°E) is east of both Indore (75.8°E) and Bhopal (77.4°E) — not between them. False. Statement 2: Aurangabad (75.3°E, 19.9°N) latitude between Vadodara (22.3°N) and Pune (18.5°N) — yes, 19.9° is between 22.3° and 18.5°. True. Statement 3: Bangalore (12.9°N) is NOT south of Chennai (13.1°N) — Bangalore is slightly south. True (barely). Answer: C (2 and 3).
factual_hooks: Jabalpur ~80°E is east of Indore (~76°E) and Bhopal (~77°E)
Aurangabad latitude (19.9°N) is between Vadodara (22.3°N) and Pune (18.5°N)
Bangalore (12.97°N) is marginally south of Chennai (13.08°N)
contrast_with: Longitude (east-west position) vs Latitude (north-south position)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse overturning (about inner vs outer wheels) and skidding direction. Centrifugal tendency pushes outward — hence vehicle skids outward and tends to overturn about outer wheels.
elimination_hint: At high speed on sharp circular path: centrifugal effect pushes outward → vehicle tends to overturn about OUTER wheels (not inner) and skids OUTWARDS. Statements 1 (overturn about outer wheels) and 3 (skid outward) are correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Centrifugal force acts outward in circular motion
Vehicle overturns about outer wheels at high speed on curve
Vehicle skids outward due to centrifugal tendency
contrast_with: Overturning about outer wheels (high speed) vs inner wheels (low speed/emergency braking on curve)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Emotional Intelligence (EI) = ability to recognize, understand and manage emotions in self and others
Daniel Goleman's model: self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills
EI developed through mindfulness, empathy training, reflective practice
contrast_with: IQ (cognitive intelligence) vs EQ (emotional intelligence) in ethical decision-making
mains_themes: Role of emotions in ethical decisions
mains_intro: Emotional Intelligence (EI) refers to the capacity to identify, understand, manage, and effectively utilize emotions in oneself and others, making it indispensable for ethical decision-making.
mains_body_points: EI encompasses five components per Goleman: self-awareness (recognizing own biases), self-regulation (controlling impulses), motivation, empathy, and social skills
EI can be developed through reflective journaling, mindfulness practices, empathy exercises, and feedback mechanisms in training programmes
A person with high EI recognizes emotional biases in decision-making, avoids knee-jerk reactions, and considers stakeholders' perspectives before acting
In ethical contexts, EI enables civil servants to balance compassion with rule-compliance, preventing both cold bureaucratic indifference and emotional bias
Historical examples: Gandhi's empathetic approach vs emotionally reactive leaders — EI as differentiator in ethical leadership
mains_conclusion: High emotional intelligence enables individuals to navigate ethical dilemmas with greater empathy, impartiality, and reflective judgment, making it a critical competency for public service.
contemporary_relevance: EI is embedded in civil service training frameworks and NEP 2020's focus on socio-emotional learning.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Gandhi's Seven Social Sins: politics without principles, wealth without work, pleasure without conscience, knowledge without character, commerce without morality, science without humanity, worship without sacrifice
Originally published in Young India (1925)
Relevance: guides public and private ethical conduct
contrast_with: Gandhi's Social Sins (systemic ethics) vs individual virtue ethics (Aristotle)
mains_intro: Mahatma Gandhi's concept of the Seven Social Sins provides a comprehensive ethical framework identifying systemic moral failures that undermine both individual character and social harmony.
mains_body_points: The seven sins are: politics without principles; wealth without work; pleasure without conscience; knowledge without character; commerce without morality; science without humanity; worship without sacrifice
Each sin identifies a dangerous uncoupling of a powerful human activity from its corresponding ethical anchor
'Politics without principles' — directly relevant to civil servants: exercise of power must be governed by constitutional morality and public service values
'Knowledge without character' highlights that technical expertise divorced from values can cause harm — relevant to policy making
These sins are not individual vices but systemic failures — Gandhi identified them as threats to civilizational integrity
Contemporary relevance: corruption ('wealth without work'), environmental destruction ('science without humanity'), religious violence ('worship without sacrifice')
mains_conclusion: Gandhi's Seven Social Sins remain a powerful ethical compass for civil servants and policymakers, reminding them that power, wealth, and knowledge must always be tempered by moral responsibility.
mains_interlinkages: GS1: Indian culture and philosophical traditions
contemporary_relevance: Gandhi's framework directly applicable to current governance challenges — corruption, regulatory capture, religious extremism.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: France also has a significant Basque population (in the Pyrénées-Atlantiques region), so France is a plausible distractor. However, the main separatist movement ETA was based in Spanish Basque Country.
elimination_hint: The Basque separatist movement (ETA) is primarily associated with Spain (Basque Country: Euskadi). While Basques also live in France, the separatist violence was centered in Spain. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Basque people: Spain (primarily) and France (lesser extent)
ETA (Euskadi Ta Askatasuna): Basque separatist organization in Spain
ETA declared permanent ceasefire 2011, disarmed 2017
contrast_with: Basque movement (Spain, ETA) vs Catalan movement (Spain, non-violent)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may forget that women choose ordered seats (arrangement, not combination) and men also arrange in ordered seats. Both require permutations (ordered selections).
elimination_hint: Women choose 2 chairs from chairs 1–4 in order: P(4,2) = 4×3 = 12. Remaining 6 chairs; men choose 3 in order: P(6,3) = 6×5×4 = 120. Total = 12 × 120 = 1440. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Women: P(4,2) = 12 ordered arrangements
Men: P(6,3) = 120 ordered arrangements
contrast_with: Permutation (chairs numbered = order matters) vs Combination (identical seats = order doesn't matter)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Tungsten is not found in Kerala beach sands — it is mined primarily in Rajasthan (Degana mine). Students may assume all heavy minerals are found in Kerala beaches.
elimination_hint: Kerala beach sands (placer deposits) contain: Ilmenite (titanium ore), Zircon, Sillimanite, Monazite, Rutile, Garnet — but NOT Tungsten. Answer: B (I, II, III only).
factual_hooks: Kerala beach sands: world-significant placer mineral deposits
Contains Ilmenite (Fe-Ti oxide), Zircon (Zr), Sillimanite (Al silicate), Monazite (Th-rare earths)
Tungsten found in Rajasthan (Degana), not Kerala beaches
contrast_with: Kerala placer minerals (beach sands) vs Rajasthan mineral deposits (ores in rock)
contemporary_relevance: Rare earth minerals in Kerala critical for India's strategic minerals policy.

subject: History
trap_factor: Amoghavarsha I was the most famous Rashtrakuta king (great patron of literature), leading students to believe he founded the dynasty. Dantidurga actually founded it.
elimination_hint: Dantidurga (r. 735–756 CE) founded the Rashtrakuta Empire by defeating the Chalukyas. Amoghavarsha I was the greatest but not the founder. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Dantidurga founded Rashtrakuta Empire ~735 CE
Amoghavarsha I (814–878 CE): greatest Rashtrakuta king, patron of Kannada literature
Rashtrakutas ruled Deccan; displaced Chalukyas of Badami
contrast_with: Dantidurga (founder) vs Amoghavarsha I (greatest ruler) — common confusion

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement II says each integer of Richter scale has 100 times the energy — students may say 'yes, I've heard that'. But the energy multiplier per unit is ~31.6 (√1000), not 100. 100 times is amplitude increase per 2 units.
elimination_hint: Statement I is correct: Richter is logarithmic, each unit = 10x amplitude. Statement II is false: energy increases by ~31.6× per unit (not 100×). Only I is correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Richter Scale: each unit = 10× amplitude increase
Each unit = ~31.6× energy increase (not 100×)
100× energy corresponds to 2 Richter units increase
contrast_with: Richter scale (amplitude-based, older) vs Moment Magnitude Scale (energy-based, modern standard)
contemporary_relevance: Earthquake risk assessment relevant given India's seismic zones.

subject: History
trap_factor: Gujarat became a state in 1960 (not 1996). Option D (Year 1996: Gujarat becomes a State) is clearly wrong — Gujarat was carved from Bombay State in 1960. Students who don't know the exact year fall for this.
elimination_hint: Gujarat became a state on May 1, 1960 (from Bombay State), NOT 1996. Option D is incorrectly matched. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Gujarat formed May 1, 1960 (with Maharashtra) from Bombay State
States Reorganization Act 1956 created Andhra Pradesh (correct)
Treaty of Yandabu 1826 ceded Assam to British (correct)
Bilaspur merged into Himachal Pradesh 1954 (correct)
contrast_with: 1956 reorganization (linguistic states) vs 1960 (Maharashtra-Gujarat split) vs later reorganizations

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse which states share borders with Nepal and Bhutan. West Bengal borders both Bhutan AND Nepal; Assam borders Bhutan but NOT Nepal directly.
elimination_hint: Assam shares borders with Bhutan and Bangladesh (not Nepal) — Statement I is correct as stated. West Bengal shares borders with Bhutan and Nepal — Statement II correct. Mizoram shares borders with Bangladesh and Myanmar — Statement III correct. All three are correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Assam: borders Bhutan (north) and Bangladesh (south) — correct
West Bengal: borders Bhutan and Nepal — correct
Mizoram: borders Bangladesh (west) and Myanmar (east) — correct
contrast_with: States bordering Nepal (West Bengal, Uttarakhand, UP, Bihar, Sikkim) vs states bordering Bhutan (Assam, West Bengal, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh)
contemporary_relevance: Border management with Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, Myanmar remains a key IR and security topic.

subject: History
trap_factor: Charter Act of 1813 abolished EIC's trade monopoly (except China and tea); Charter Act of 1853 abolished EIC's patronage but not trade monopoly (that was already gone). Statement I is therefore false.
elimination_hint: EIC's trade monopoly was abolished by Charter Act of 1813 (not 1853). Charter Act of 1853 made civil services open to competition. Statement I is false. Statement II (Government of India Act 1858 abolished EIC) is correct. Answer: B (II only).
factual_hooks: Charter Act 1813: abolished EIC trade monopoly (except China tea)
Charter Act 1853: introduced open competition for civil services, no trade provisions
Government of India Act 1858: Crown took over from EIC; first Viceroy appointed
contrast_with: Charter Act 1813 (trade monopoly abolished) vs Charter Act 1853 (civil service reform)
